focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The dean's office was cold in the kind of mean way only school buildings managed in June, as if the air-conditioning existed less to cool anybody than to remind kids which rooms belonged to adults. Micah sat under a row of faded college pennants with his knees open and his jaw loose like none of this mattered. He kept his backpack hooked around one sneaker so the shaking in his leg did not travel into the whole chair.

Across from him, the trophy case shone with old football helmets and dust trapped behind glass. Every time the outer door opened, a rectangle of heat fell across the linoleum, then got swallowed by the office chill. Kids passed the doorway in clumps, loud and alive and headed somewhere that was not here. A couple of them looked in. Micah lifted his chin at them like he had chosen the chair and the wait and the white envelope clipped under his mother's thumb.

Marcia sat beside him in a navy blouse already darkened under the arms from the walk over. She had changed out of her church skirt and into work pants, but she still looked arranged, every button where it belonged, every edge under control. Her handbag sat upright on her lap. She held herself perfectly still, phone buried, questions spent. She had gone past talking.

That was worse than the talking would have been.

If she had wanted to cuss him out, he knew the shape of that weather. She would have gotten exact, then sharp, then loud enough to make the room understand she had not raised some wild little idiot for school people to pin to a bulletin board. Instead she sat with her back straight and her mouth fixed, as if whatever she needed from him would be collected later, in private, with interest.

Micah scratched at the healing split in his knuckle and stopped when she turned her hea...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...vette's torn receipt went inside. The June line was marked paid. The handwriting looked thin and stubborn on the page.

Then she opened the drawer by the stove and took out the light bill.

The red band across the top shouted in a font that was almost cheerful. She laid it beside the notebook on the table. Under the clear plastic, the edge of Tiana's housing contract still showed if a person knew where to look. There were three claims there now: Yvette's thin promise of July, the light company's red threat of Tuesday, and August first waiting under Tiana's name. They all wanted the same hand, the same wallet, the same woman to answer first.

Outside, Delroy laughed at something on his phone, some man joke floating up warm through mosquito hum. From the bed area, Micah said, "Nah, he wish," and Tiana told him to shut up if he was going to keep lying that loudly. Their voices tangled, sharp and ordinary. It was family noise. It was life noise. It made the room sound held together when it was mostly held up.

Marcia stacked the notebook on top of the bill, then stood there with both palms resting on the covers as if she could press the whole stack into mercy. The electric money was already in Yvette's hand. The city's red slip still sat folded in her purse. Micah's meeting was at 8:15. Tiana had hidden an August first deposit line under the table where people usually stored coupons and church flyers.

Sunday had not stayed ordinary. It had only dressed itself that way long enough to get through the door.

She slid the notebook back into the cupboard and left the light bill flat in the middle of the table where nobody leaving at dawn could miss it. July had to come now. She had paid for that future with Tuesday's current, and there was no getting either one back by wishing.
